elements.sent-2411-001: From a set of uncalibrated images (a) we extract 2D detections of static urban objects (b), and generate local top-down 2D maps representing the spatial arrangement of the objects with respect to each image (c).

elements.sent-2411-003: World-wide detailed 2D maps require enormous collective efforts.
elements.sent-2411-004: OpenStreetMap is the result of 11 million registered users manually annotating the GPS location of over 1.75 billion entries, including distinctive landmarks and common urban objects.
elements.sent-2411-005: At the same time, manual annotations can include errors and are slow to update, limiting the map's accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-006: Maps from Motion (MfM) is a step forward to automatize such time-consuming map making procedure by computing 2D maps of semantic objects directly from a collection of uncalibrated multi-view images.
elements.sent-2411-007: From each image, we extract a set of object detections, and estimate their spatial arrangement in a top-down local map centered in the reference frame of the camera that captured the image.
elements.sent-2411-008: Aligning these local maps is not a trivial problem, since they provide incomplete, noisy fragments of the scene, and matching detections across them is unreliable because of the presence of repeated pattern and the limited appearance variability of urban objects.
elements.sent-2411-009: We address this with a novel graph-based framework, that encodes the spatial and semantic distribution of the objects detected in each image, and learns how to combine them to predict the objects' poses in a global reference system, while taking into account all possible detection matches and preserving the topology observed in each image.
elements.sent-2411-010: Despite the complexity of the problem, our best model achieves global 2D registration with an average accuracy within $4$ meters (\ie below GPS accuracy) even on sparse sequences with strong viewpoint change, on which COLMAP has an $80\%$ failure rate.
elements.sent-2411-011: We provide extensive evaluation on synthetic and real-world data, showing how the method obtains a solution even in scenarios where standard optimization techniques fail.
elements.sent-2411-012: In most public spaces, like museums and parks, we are used to finding our way via minimalist maps that mark our current location as a red, ``You are here \textcolor{red {\faMapMarker '' dot.}}

elements.sent-2411-068: MfM addresses the problem of generating a 2D global map from a set of sparse and wide-baseline images.
elements.sent-2411-069: As illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:themethod , this is done across three main steps.}
elements.sent-2411-070: First, the images are turned into local maps (Figure~\ref{fig:themethod .a), representing the spatial arrangement of the objects detected in the images and their classes.}
elements.sent-2411-071: These maps are obtained by reprojecting the center of the object detections in 3D and then projecting it on the ground plane, as detailed in Section~\ref{sec:method.problem_setup .}
elements.sent-2411-072: MfM combines the local maps by forming a graph (Figure~\ref{fig:themethod .b), in which the detections are represented as nodes, the local maps as subgraphs, and the cross-map connections as edges (Section~\ref{sec:method.graph_representation ).}}
elements.sent-2411-073: We then train a GNN to aggregate the information and regress the location of each detected object in the reference system of a global map (Figure~\ref{fig:themethod .c), while preserving the spatial layout of each local map.}
elements.sent-2411-074: Given a set of $K$ images $\mathcal{I =\{I_i\}_{i\in[1,\dots,K]}$, we estimate a set of local maps $\mathcal{M = \{M_i\}_{i\in[1,\dots,K]}$.}}

elements.sent-2411-076: This results in a detection $o_{ij}$ with semantic label $l_{ij}$ for each object $j$ observed in the image.

elements.sent-2411-078: Finally, we represent the coordinates of the detection in the image as a bounding box $b_{ij}$.
elements.sent-2411-079: The use of monocular depth estimation and averaging the depth of the whole detection into a single point will introduce some noise, and for this reason we also evaluate the proposed approach using perfect depth (Sec.~\ref{sec:experiments:sfmo ) and perform ablation tests on the effect of noise on the local maps (Sec.~\ref{sec:experiments:ablations ).}}
elements.sent-2411-080: Finally, we generate the local maps by reprojecting the center of each bounding box in 3D, and then projecting it onto the scene's ground plane.

elements.sent-2411-181: In the paper, we propose a solution to the novel task of MfM, \ie generating 2D object maps by fusing into a global reference frame a set of local 2D semantic maps, representing the partial 2D map as observed from their view point.
elements.sent-2411-182: This initial method provides a first step towards developing tools to automatically generate annotations on 2D maps from uncalibrated images, without having to generate a 3D reconstruction of the scene or establish matches between the images.
elements.sent-2411-183: We shown how the proposed approach, MfM, can provide accurate maps even in the presence of limited information, such as noisy input maps and no availability of cross-view match between the detected objects.
elements.sent-2411-184: Even in these challenging scenarios, MfM provides an average localization within GPS accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-185: Moreover, when applied to sequences of very sparse images (e.g. five) with large viewpoint changes, MfM achieves approximately three times lower failure rate than COLMAP.
elements.sent-2411-186: The main limitation of the approach, as demonstrated in the ablation experiments, lies in its dependency on objects' covisibility among the multi-view images.
elements.sent-2411-187: Based on empirical estimations, every image must share at least three detections with another view.
elements.sent-2411-188: Images containing only classes not observed in the other images will result in disconnected subgraphs in $\tilde{\mathcal{G }$, making the alignment impossible.}
elements.sent-2411-189: Future work will expand the approach to predict accurate detection matches across maps, to improve the information aggregation capabilities of graph-based learning models.
elements.sent-2411-190: This will allow extending the approach to tasks like multi-view object localization.
elements.sent-2411-191: Additionally, depth estimation is not the most accurate way for generating top-view maps, and was used to provide a lower-accuracy limit; future work will employ more sophisticated approaches, like generating segmented BEV maps from each image.

elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth, trim={0 1cm 2.5cm 3cm}, clip] {Images/mapmaker_maptograph_final4.pdf} \caption{Graph Formulation for the MfM problem. Given a set of local maps, we {\em a)} assign to each map annotation a node in the graph and {\em b)} draw intra-map edges to generate a complete subgraph for each map. We then {\em c)} draw inter-map edges connecting detections matched to the same object. Finally, we {\em d)} assign to each node of the graph an embedding defined by concatenating the detection's coordinates in the corresponding local map, the one-hot encoding of its semantic class, and the location of a bounding box fitted to the segmentation mask.} \label{fig:thegraph}
elements.sent-2411-194: In Section 3.3 of the main paper, we introduce a Self-Consistency Loss that requires finding the optimal transformation aligning two maps, \ie two sets of 2D points, in the same reference frame.
elements.sent-2411-195: To train the model, we need this loss to be differentiable, with stable gradients and efficient to compute.
elements.sent-2411-196: To satisfy these requirements, we introduce a novel alignment algorithm constrained to a 2D surface and inspired by the Procrustes algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-197: Consider two 2D points $c_i$ and $c_j$ where $c_i, c_j\in \mathcal{R ^{N\times 2}$, with the same number of elements and sorted such that the $n$-th element of $c_i$ and $c_j$ represent the same object but in different reference frames.}
elements.sent-2411-198: These 2D point arrangements can be aligned by finding the rigid transformation defined by a rotation angle $\theta$, a scaling factor $\lambda$, and a translation $\tau$ that solves the problem: \begin{equation}\Theta(c_i,c_j) = \argmin_{\theta,\tau, \lambda} \| c_i - (\lambda R(\theta) \cdot c_j + \tau) \|. \label{eq:align2d}\end{equation} To find this transformation, we first center and normalize the two point clouds, to reduce the alignment problem to estimating a rotation around the cloud's centers: \begin{equation}\tau_i = \frac{1}{N}\sum c_i \tau_j = \frac{1}{N}\sum c_j \lambda_i = \|c_i - \mu_i\| \lambda_j = \|c_j - \mu_j\| c'_i = \frac{c_i - \tau_i}{\lambda_i} c'_j = \frac{c_j - \tau_j}{\lambda_j}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-199: We can then compare the angular distribution of points in $c'_i$ and $c'_j$, to estimate the rotation angle $\theta_{ji}$ that best aligns them.
elements.sent-2411-200: The orientation of point $l$ in the point cloud $c'_i$, $\theta_l^i$, can be obtained as $\gamma_l^i=\|c^l_i\|^{-1 c^l_i=[\cos(\theta_l^i), \sin(\theta_l^i)]$, \ie by normalizing the vector connecting the point to the center of the cloud $\mu_k$.}
elements.sent-2411-201: The two angular distributions are found as $\gamma_i = \left\{ [\cos(\theta_l^i), \sin(\theta_l^i)] \right\}_{l \in c'_i}$ and $\gamma_j = \left\{ [\cos(\theta_l^j), \sin(\theta_l^j)] \right\}_{l \in c'_j}$.
elements.sent-2411-202: Due to the possible presence of noise in the input 2D point clouds, we compute the average angular distance between pairs of matched points to find the rotation angle $\theta_{ji}$ mapping the two distributions.
elements.sent-2411-203: This is performed using the Prosthaphaeresis formulas, which can be computed as follows: \begin{equation}\sin{\theta_{ji}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{l} (\sin{\theta_j^l}\cos{\theta_i^l} - \cos{\theta_j^l}\sin{\theta_i^l}) \cos{\theta_{ji}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{l} (\cos{\theta_j^l}\cos{\theta_i^l} - \sin{\theta_j^l}\sin{\theta_i^l}) R(\theta_{ji}) =  \cos{\theta_{ji}} \sin{\theta_{ji}} -\sin{\theta_{ji}} \cos{\theta_{ji}} .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-204: The rotation $R(\theta_{ji )$, the scaling factor $\lambda_i$ and the translation $\tau_i$ then define the optimal transformation mapping $c_j$ onto $c_i$, \ie $c_i \approx \lambda_i R(\theta_{ji ) \cdot c_j + \tau_i$.}}

elements.sent-2411-207: In Section 3.3 of the main paper, we propose applying a combination of three loss functions (Euclidean Camera-Object Pose, Cross-Map Consistency, and Self-Similarity) to train the GNN-based Alignment Module.
